ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers waffles generically; no dedicated Hong Kong egg waffle subcategory exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole Hong Kong egg waffle typically weighs approximately 150 g and is sold as a single street-food portion consisting of multiple bubble-shaped cells joined together.

nutrient_source.title: Hong Kong Egg Waffle — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are preserved as supplied. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for plain waffles (USDA SR Legacy #18370) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hong Kong egg waffle (gai daan jai) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 76 based on the closest analogous food: plain waffle made from refined wheat flour with sugar, which has a published GI of approximately 76 (University of Sydney GI Database; waffles, Aunt Jemima, GI=76). The high sugar content (21.6 g/100g) and low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g) of this product are consistent with a high-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (76 × 75.6) / 100 = 57.5 for a 150 g serving (carbohydrate_g = 50.4 × 1.5 = 75.6 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (21.6 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium is moderate at 128 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Hong Kong egg waffle consumption because its high sugar load (21.6 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and fluid retention, which can elevate blood pressure independently of dietary sodium intake; if consumed, restrict to a small portion (≤50 g) and avoid sweetened toppings such as condensed milk or chocolate sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 21.6 g/100g exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate 50.4 g/100g with only 0.7 g/100g dietary fibre; estimated GI 76 (high); glycemic load 57.5 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hong Kong egg waffle is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes because its sugar content (21.6 g/100g) exceeds the 20 g/100g hard threshold, its high GI (~76) and very low fibre (0.7 g/100g) produce rapid and pronounced postprandial blood glucose spikes, and a single 150 g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 57.5; this food should be avoided or, at most, consumed in very small occasional portions with close blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar (21.6 g/100g) promotes dyslipidaemia and triglyceride elevation; saturated fat estimated at 3.5 g/100g from butter/margarine base; high-heat cooking generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Hong Kong egg waffle only occasionally and in small portions because its high sugar content (21.6 g/100g) raises triglycerides and promotes atherogenic dyslipidaemia, its saturated fat content (~3.5 g/100g) contributes to LDL elevation, and high-heat waffle-iron cooking generates AGEs that promote vascular inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hong Kong egg waffle (雞蛋仔 / 鸡蛋仔, Cantonese: gai daan jai) is a popular Hong Kong street food consisting of a grid of small, spherical, bubble-shaped cells formed by cooking an egg-rich batter in a specially designed round waffle iron. The batter is made from wheat flour, eggs, sugar, evaporated milk, butter or margarine, custard powder, and baking powder, producing a snack that is crispy on the outside and soft, slightly eggy, and chewy on the inside. It is typically served hot, folded in half, and eaten as a handheld snack. Per 100 g, it provides 316 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 9.2 g fat, 50.4 g carbohydrate, 21.6 g sugar, 0.7 g dietary fibre, and 128 mg sodium. The high sugar content (21.6 g/100g) and low fibre (0.7 g/100g) give it a high estimated glycemic index (~76) and a very high glycemic load (~57.5 per 150 g serving), making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The sugar load also raises cardiovascular and hypertension risk through triglyceride elevation and insulin-mediated sodium retention. High-heat cooking in the waffle iron generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory. This food is widely sold at street stalls and hawker centres in Hong Kong and Singapore and is a culturally significant Cantonese snack enjoyed across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy plain waffle data and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest published waffle GI value, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain waffles (#18370, 'Waffles, plain, frozen, ready-to-heat') as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This is an approximation; actual saturated fat may vary depending on the butter-to-margarine ratio used by individual vendors. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Hong Kong egg waffle are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a direct entry as of 2026. 3. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on plain waffle (Aunt Jemima) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for gai daan jai exists in any approved source. 4. The input health_suitability entries for 'diabetes' and 'hypertension' have been remapped to 'type2_diabetes' and 'hypertension' respectively per Veritas naming rules. The 'rheumatoid_arthritis' condition from the input has been moved to additional_conditions and is not included in the mandatory three conditions. 5. This food is classified as a Singaporean-context dish sold widely in Singapore hawker centres; multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are therefore provided per G6 rules.
